ThinkPHP Day 8: What MVC Actually Means (and Why You Should Care)

Before we get into the later chapters, I have to pump the brakes for a second and say sorry — I'm stalling again. The thing is, we really need to understand what the MVC (Model-View-Controller) architecture is first. So this chapter is going to give you a quick intro to what MVC actually is, and trust me, this concept makes all the development we do later a whole lot clearer.
First off, each letter in MVC stands for something:
Back in the early days of development, before anyone had the MVC idea, the common approach was to cram everything into a single
A piece of my dark history, please don't judge me QQ

The problem is, once a system starts getting big, this becomes a nightmare to maintain and develop. You've got all your PHP code and business logic mashed together, not to mention the HTML thrown in there too. Come back to this code a few months later and honestly there's a good chance you'll be sitting there thinking "WT...? I wrote this??"
So to fix this, a guy named
came up with the MVC software architecture pattern. The main idea is to split your code into Model, View, and Controller, so each part can just focus on doing its own job well.
Model — this is mainly for working with data. Anything related to data operations, just toss it in here.
View — basically the page that gets shown to the user (HTML, CSS, JS).
Controller — this one plays the role of a kind of middleman. It mainly talks to the Model and the View, telling them which data the user needs and which screen to show.
So how does MVC actually work in practice? You might think the user requests a page from the View first (since it's the "view," right?), but actually that's
how it goes — the user requests which screen they want from the Controller first.
If we put it in human terms, it goes something like this:

User: Hey~ I want the homepage!
Controller: Oh! You want the homepage.

Controller: Hey View! The user says they want the homepage, go serve it up!
View: Sure thing~
(User receives the homepage)
Now you might ask: "So what about the Model?"
If the screen the user requested needs to read data from the database, then the Controller tells the Model which View to hand the data off to, and then it gets sent back to the user to display.
Let's run through that in human terms again:

User: Hey~ I want the product list page!
Controller: Got it, you want the product list page.

Controller: View, I need the product list page, then send it to the user.
View: Product list data, huh — but I need to grab the data from the Model first.

View: Hey Model! I need the product list data, send it over.
Model: Ok, here's the product list data.
(View serves the page back to the user)
That's roughly the flow. If we look at it as a diagram it'd look like this, but these days everything goes through a Route, so there's an extra Route step that makes it a little different ↓
And the most important core part is right in the middle — that's the MVC ↓
The dark part is the MVC core
Of course there's a lot of pretty complex logic that can live in here too, and that business logic usually gets pulled out into its own separate
. This series won't cover that, but the reason you'd break out a
is mainly to keep your code from getting messy and to make it more reusable.

Once you've got these basic concepts down, the directory structure in the later chapters will make a lot more sense, and you'll have a clear idea of which folder — Model, View, or Controller — to drop things into.
Finally, if you want to dig deeper into some related concepts, I'd recommend this article for further reading:
跟著小明一起搞懂技術名詞：MVC、SPA 與 SSR

ThinkPHP itself uses the MVC architecture, and it also supports the MVVM architecture. But what I'm going with here is MVC, since it's a super classic example architecture and still really common in the industry. If you've already got a decent grasp of MVC, then MVVM won't be a big deal for you either.

Before we get into the later chapters, I have to pump the brakes for a second and say sorry — we need to understand the MVC (Model-View-Controller) pattern first. This chapter walks through what MVC actually is, because the concept makes everything we build later way easier to follow. We'll go through what each letter means, how the flow actually works, and why splitting your code this way saves you from future pain.
